BLOG.HERO.TITLE: Production-Ready Microservices Architecture
BLOG.HERO.SUBTITLE: Static content on AWS, /api traffic to on-premise Kubernetes and decoupled services behind an Ingress.
BLOG.STATUS: 🚧 Architecture in evolution — aligned with the target diagram
BLOG.INTRO.TEXT: The platform separates frontend delivery from the business runtime. Users enter through the public domain, receive static assets from AWS, and only API requests travel to the Kubernetes cluster.
BLOG.DIAGRAM.TITLE: Architecture diagram
BLOG.DIAGRAM.CAPTION: AWS serves static content, Route53 and CloudFront distribute public traffic, and Kubernetes handles the microservices layer.
BLOG.TECH.AWS.TITLE: AWS Cloud
BLOG.TECH.AWS.DESC: Public layer used to serve the domain, frontend and static assets.
BLOG.TECH.AWS.USE: Provides global distribution, caching and a clean entry point for static content.
BLOG.TECH.INGRESS.TITLE: Nginx Ingress
BLOG.TECH.INGRESS.DESC: Cluster entry point that routes /api traffic to internal services.
BLOG.TECH.INGRESS.USE: Centralizes HTTP access and keeps public exposure separate from microservices.
BLOG.TECH.KUBERNETES.TITLE: Kubernetes
BLOG.TECH.KUBERNETES.DESC: Runtime environment for the business layer and its independent services.
BLOG.TECH.KUBERNETES.USE: Orchestrates deployment, scaling and availability of the microservices.
BLOG.TECH.MICROSERVICES.TITLE: Microservices
BLOG.TECH.MICROSERVICES.DESC: Separate services for user, cart, product, order and mail.
BLOG.TECH.MICROSERVICES.USE: Each service owns a focused responsibility and can evolve independently.
BLOG.TECH.AUTH.TITLE: Hybrid Auth
BLOG.TECH.AUTH.DESC: Hybrid authentication with local database users and Google as an external provider.
BLOG.TECH.AUTH.USE: Combines internal user control with a social login flow.
BLOG.TECH.CONFIG.TITLE: Secrets and App Config
BLOG.TECH.CONFIG.DESC: Sensitive configuration and environment settings kept outside the codebase.
BLOG.TECH.CONFIG.USE: Reduces coupling and keeps deployments consistent across environments.
BLOG.FLOW.TITLE: Architecture Flow
BLOG.FLOW.TEXT: The user enters through the public domain, receives static content from AWS, and /api requests go through the Ingress to the services running in the cluster. Hybrid auth and external configuration complete the path.
BLOG.ROADMAP.TITLE: Project Status
BLOG.ROADMAP.ITEM1: ✔ Public delivery of static assets on AWS
BLOG.ROADMAP.ITEM4: ✔ Hybrid authentication with Google and database
BLOG.ROADMAP.ITEM5: ✔ Configuration through Secrets and App Config
BLOG.OVERVIEW.TITLE: Modular and extensible reference architecture
BLOG.OVERVIEW.TEXT: This project demonstrates how to build a real microservices ecosystem with a polished frontend. It is not a simple code example, but an educational foundation that integrates business, infrastructure and integration.
BLOG.BACKEND.TITLE: Server-side services, domain and capabilities
BLOG.BACKEND.SUBTITLE: Each service owns a specific responsibility and communicates through REST, Feign, Kafka and centralized configuration.
BLOG.BACKEND.USER.TITLE: User service
BLOG.BACKEND.USER.DESC: Handles local authentication, Google login, user profile, roles, refresh tokens and event analytics.
BLOG.BACKEND.USER.NOTE: Includes secure-cookie JWTs, OAuth2, Envers auditing and analytics summary endpoints.
BLOG.BACKEND.PRODUCT.TITLE: Product service
BLOG.BACKEND.PRODUCT.DESC: Exposes catalog browsing, search, categories and batch access to enrich cart and orders.
BLOG.BACKEND.PRODUCT.NOTE: Built for pagination, simple filtering and internal Feign consumption.
BLOG.BACKEND.CART.TITLE: Cart service
BLOG.BACKEND.CART.DESC: Keeps a cart per user, lazily creates it and validates products before performing operations.
BLOG.BACKEND.CART.NOTE: Acts as the transactional state for checkout and stores lightweight product snapshots.
BLOG.BACKEND.ORDER.TITLE: Order service
BLOG.BACKEND.ORDER.DESC: Converts the cart into orders, manages statuses, history, admin actions and mock payment.
BLOG.BACKEND.ORDER.NOTE: Adds webhook idempotency, Kafka event publishing and RBAC-controlled admin flows.
BLOG.BACKEND.MAIL.TITLE: Mail service
BLOG.BACKEND.MAIL.DESC: Consumes user and payment events to send asynchronous emails without coupling them to the main flow.
BLOG.BACKEND.MAIL.NOTE: Ideal for onboarding and order confirmation notifications via Kafka listeners.
BLOG.BACKEND.CORE.TITLE: Core shared module
BLOG.BACKEND.CORE.DESC: Bundles DTOs, JWT security, exceptions, OpenAPI and shared utilities used by all services.
BLOG.BACKEND.CORE.NOTE: Reduces duplication and keeps authentication, mapping and responses consistent.
BLOG.FRONTEND.TITLE: Angular architecture, UX and visual composition
BLOG.FRONTEND.SUBTITLE: The app is structured with standalone components, lazy routes, translation pipes and lightweight global state.
BLOG.FRONTEND.ROUTING.TITLE: Routing and lazy loading
BLOG.FRONTEND.ROUTING.DESC: Each screen is loaded on demand, separating landing, auth, dashboard, catalog, cart, orders and admin.
BLOG.FRONTEND.ROUTING.NOTE: Keeps the initial bundle small and navigation costs low.
BLOG.FRONTEND.STATE.TITLE: State and session
BLOG.FRONTEND.STATE.DESC: UserStateService and CartService hold global state and allow session/cart rehydration after login.
BLOG.FRONTEND.STATE.NOTE: Guards consume this state and redirect without exposing the token to the browser.
BLOG.FRONTEND.UI.TITLE: Visual system
BLOG.FRONTEND.UI.DESC: PrimeNG cards, tables, badges and panels with a consistent, more editorial visual language.
BLOG.FRONTEND.UI.NOTE: The interface prioritizes hierarchy, readability and state cues.
BLOG.FRONTEND.I18N.TITLE: Centralized translation
BLOG.FRONTEND.I18N.DESC: Every screen uses a translation pipe and JSON locale dictionaries.
BLOG.FRONTEND.I18N.NOTE: Lets you sell the product across markets without touching the base markup.
BLOG.FRONTEND.AUTH.TITLE: Client-side authentication
BLOG.FRONTEND.AUTH.DESC: Startup calls /me, fills global state and manages user/admin permissions.
BLOG.FRONTEND.AUTH.NOTE: The frontend never stores JWTs in localStorage; it relies on secure cookies.
BLOG.FRONTEND.SCREENS.TITLE: Business screens
BLOG.FRONTEND.SCREENS.DESC: Home, catalog, cart, orders, user detail, order detail and admin management share the same visual language.
BLOG.FRONTEND.SCREENS.NOTE: The experience feels like a single app even though it is split into independent features.
BLOG.FLOWS.TITLE: End-to-end journeys that explain the platform value
BLOG.FLOWS.SUBTITLE: These are the flows that help the most when selling the solution because they connect business, integration and infrastructure.
BLOG.FLOWS.AUTH.TITLE: Login and session
BLOG.FLOWS.AUTH.DESC: Users sign in locally or with Google, receive a secure session and the frontend rehydrates state on startup.
BLOG.FLOWS.CATALOG.TITLE: Browsing and cart
BLOG.FLOWS.CATALOG.DESC: The catalog paginates products, the cart keeps per-user quantities and actions update the UI immediately.
BLOG.FLOWS.CHECKOUT.TITLE: Checkout and payment
BLOG.FLOWS.CHECKOUT.DESC: The order is created from the cart, the mock payment triggers the webhook and Kafka fans out the event.
BLOG.FLOWS.ADMIN.TITLE: Operations and governance
BLOG.FLOWS.ADMIN.DESC: Admins manage users, orders and analytics from separate screens with RBAC and restricted actions.
BLOG.HIGHLIGHTS.TITLE: Technical decisions that matter
BLOG.HIGHLIGHTS.SUBTITLE: These architectural patterns are what distinguish a production system from an educational prototype.
BLOG.HIGHLIGHT.IDEMPOTENCY.TITLE: Idempotency in orders
BLOG.HIGHLIGHT.IDEMPOTENCY.DESC: The order service guarantees that a duplicate webhook does not create two orders. It uses a unique database constraint and AOP for deduplication control.
BLOG.HIGHLIGHT.AUDIT.TITLE: Audit trail with Envers
BLOG.HIGHLIGHT.AUDIT.DESC: Every user and role change is automatically recorded. It allows tracking who did what and when, critical for compliance.
BLOG.HIGHLIGHT.ASYNC.TITLE: Asynchronous messaging with Kafka
BLOG.HIGHLIGHT.ASYNC.DESC: User and payment events are published in a decoupled way. The mail service consumes events without coupling to the main flow.
BLOG.CLOSING.TITLE: What to highlight in the README and demo
BLOG.CLOSING.SUBTITLE: This base already speaks product, architecture and operations; it only needs stronger narrative packaging.
BLOG.CLOSING.ITEM1: Public frontend on AWS and business services in Kubernetes behind Ingress.
BLOG.CLOSING.ITEM2: Hybrid auth, roles and secure cookies for a realistic user experience.
BLOG.CLOSING.ITEM3: Idempotency, Kafka and async mail for operational resilience.
BLOG.CLOSING.ITEM4: Audit, OpenAPI and shared DTOs for a maintainable backend.
BLOG.CLOSING.ITEM5: Modern Angular with SSR, lazy routes and full translation.
BLOG.CLOSING.ITEM6: Careful UI work to turn the repository into a convincing commercial demo.
